solution.p: We will factor y^{2}+y-90 where a=1, b=1, and c=-90.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For y^{2}+y-90, since the product of 10 and -9 is -90, and the sum of 10 and -9 is 1, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+y-90\amp =\left(y+10\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-9\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (-90) = -90.

The number -90 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(-90)\amp -1(90)\amp 2(-45)\amp -2(45)
ewline3(-30)\amp -3(30)\amp 5(-18)\amp -5(18)
ewline6(-15)\amp -6(15)\amp 9(-10)\amp -9(10)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 1, and this sum happens with (-9)+10=1 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+y-90\amp =y^{2}-9y+10y-90
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y-9ight)+10\mathopen{}\left(y-9ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y+10ight)\mathopen{}\left(y-9ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}+y-90 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}

The third term, -90, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{llll}1(-90)\amp -1(90)\amp 2(-45)\amp -2(45)
ewline3(-30)\amp -3(30)\amp 5(-18)\amp -5(18)
ewline6(-15)\amp -6(15)\amp 9(-10)\amp -9(10)
ewline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}+y-90 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+y-90\amp =\left(y+10\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-9\right)\end{aligned}
